lesson.name: College Success and Information Research - Lesson 4: College Research Foundations

tasks.chapterTitle: Introduction to Research and the Information Landscape
Part I: Welcome and Research Concepts
The Scholarly Conversation, Citations, and Plagiarism
Part II: Foundations of Academic Research
Anatomy of a Journal Article and Peer Review
Other Source Types and Government Information
Part III: Exploring the Information Landscape
Part IV-VI: Source Evaluation, History of Libraries, and the Information Economy
Part VII-VIII: Generative AI and Citation Management

messages.content:
Based on the document provided, here is a summary of the foundational concepts for academic research:

### **Foundations of Academic Research**
Research is an active process of inquiry involving brainstorming, searching, evaluating, and synthesizing information. It is a complex landscape influenced by historical, social, and economic structures.

#### **1. The Six Stages of the Research Process**
*   **Topic Selection:** Defining the area of study; expect this to evolve as you search.
*   **Background Research:** Using tools like encyclopedias to understand terminology and context.
*   **Finding Sources:** Exploring different source types and using library search tools.
*   **Evaluation:** Determining the trustworthiness and relevance of a source.
*   **Synthesis:** Connecting sources to support your own unique argument or voice.
*   **Citations:** Giving credit to the work of others; this is mandatory and essential for the scholarly conversation.

#### **2. Understanding Source Types**
*   **Popular Sources:** Targeted at the general public; designed to entertain, inform, or persuade.
*   **Scholarly Sources:** Written by academic experts for an audience of other scholars; dedicated to communicating original research.
*   **Context Matters:** A source’s value depends on the researcher's specific need. Even a "non-scholarly" source can be a valid object of study in certain research contexts.

#### **3. The Scholarly Conversation**
*   Research is an ongoing dialogue. By using outside sources, you join this conversation.
*   Maintaining this dialogue requires ethical practices, specifically **proper citation** to avoid **plagiarism**, which is the act of claiming another’s work as your own.

#### **4. Evaluating Expertise**
*   While there are many forms of expertise (lived, professional, etc.), academic research primarily focuses on **academic expertise**, characterized by advanced degrees and formal research experience in a field of study.

*(Reference: [S.19], [S.20], [S.21], [S.22], [S.24], [S.33], [S.34])*

Lesson 4: College Research Foundations - Presentation6 slides
College Research Foundations
  • Navigate academic research processes
  • Learn to evaluate sources critically

This document serves as the open textbook for the course "INTDSC 1010: Research and the Information Landscape" at the University of Missouri-St. Louis, authored by librarians Libby Wheeles and Helena Marvin [S.9].

The textbook is structured into eight thematic parts designed to guide undergraduates through the complexities of academic research [S.5, S.6, S.7, S.8]:

Part I: Welcome and Introduction to Scholarly Conversation
Covers the fundamental definition of research, the concept of scholarly conversation, and resources available at UMSL Libraries [S.5].

Part II: Foundations of Academic Research
Focuses on distinguishing between popular and scholarly sources, understanding the anatomy of journal articles, and the peer review process [S.5, S.6].

Part III: Exploring the Information Landscape
Addresses search strategies, including the use of Google, library databases, and tools like MOBIUS/ILL, alongside methods for generating citations [S.6].

Part IV: Source Evaluation
Introduces the SIFT method for web evaluation, guidelines for evaluating scholarly expertise, and the concept of neutrality [S.6].

Part V: History of Libraries & Information
Explores information practices before and during the internet age and how information formats influence content [S.6, S.7].

Part VI: The Information Economy
Examines scholarly publishing, the impact of paywalls, Open Access, copyright, and Creative Commons licensing [S.7].

Part VII: Generative AI
Discusses key concepts, practical limitations, and ethical considerations regarding the use of AI in research environments [S.7].

Part VIII: Citation Management
Provides a practical introduction to using Zotero for managing research references [S.8].

The course aims to equip students with the ability to evaluate sources, navigate research tools, understand the history of scholarly communication, and contribute to academic discourse [S.9].

This section introduces the fundamentals of academic research and the UMSL library environment.

### Course Overview and Objectives
The primary goal of this course is to provide students with essential research skills, including navigating library resources, evaluating sources, and understanding the "information landscape." By the end of the week, students should be able to explain the library’s role at UMSL, distinguish between paywalled and open content, and understand the core steps of the research process [S.13].

### The Research Process
Research is defined as an active process of discovering knowledge through brainstorming, searching, evaluation, and synthesis rather than a static discovery [S.19]. The process consists of six iterative steps:
1. Choosing and refining a topic [S.20].
2. Acquiring background information [S.20].
3. Finding appropriate sources [S.20].
4. Evaluating the trustworthiness and relevance of sources [S.20].
5. Synthesizing information to support original arguments [S.20].
6. Citing sources to acknowledge previous intellectual contributions [S.20].

### The Scholarly Conversation and Citations
Academic research is described as an ongoing "scholarly conversation" where experts build upon each other's work. By utilizing outside sources in their assignments, students contribute to this dialogue [S.22]. Citations are a non-optional requirement of this process, as they give credit to the original creators, allow readers to locate sources, and prevent plagiarism [S.22].

### Instructors and Resources
The course is taught by UMSL reference librarians Libby Wheeles and Helena “Lena” Marvin. The course materials are provided as Open Educational Resources (OER), meaning they are freely accessible and redistributable under a Creative Commons license [S.15, S.17, S.18].

This section provides an overview of the scholarly conversation, the importance of proper citation, and the consequences of plagiarism. 

The core concepts are as follows:

The Scholarly Conversation: Researchers contribute to a collective body of knowledge by sharing their findings, which others then build upon or critique. Citations are a critical mechanism in this process because they allow authors to acknowledge the specific sources that inform their work (p. 24).

Plagiarism: Defined as claiming another individual's work as one's own or using outside information without providing proper credit, plagiarism is considered a serious academic offense. Engaging in plagiarism can lead to a loss of credit or more severe disciplinary actions from a university (p. 23).

Practical Guidance: Students are encouraged to utilize academic resources—such as professors, librarians, and writing centers—if they are uncertain about whether their work constitutes plagiarism. Learning to cite sources correctly is a fundamental skill that will be developed throughout the course (p. 23).

The UMSL Library system serves as a central hub for research, learning, and study support. Its primary objectives are to facilitate access to high-quality information, provide expert guidance on research methods, and offer dedicated spaces for academic work (p. S.25, S.26).

Key components of the UMSL library system include:

1. Materials and Access: The library offers a vast collection ranging from books and journals to specialized digital databases. While many academic resources are protected by paywalls that require individual fees, the library provides access to these materials at no cost to students. Furthermore, the library supports the use of Open Access (OA) content and Open Educational Resources (OER) to promote affordable education (p. S.26, S.27).

2. Facility Structure: The UMSL campus houses three distinct libraries within one common building:
- Thomas Jefferson (TJ) Library: The primary library for general research, hosting reference librarians, study spaces, and research assistance (p. S.27).
- The St. Louis Mercantile Library: A specialized research library established in 1846, focusing on Western Expansion and the history of St. Louis and transportation (p. S.28, S.29).
- State Historical Society – St. Louis: Located on level two, this center provides unique collections documenting the history and evolution of the St. Louis region (p. S.29).

3. Research Services: Students can utilize several support channels, including:
- Research Appointments: One-on-one sessions with librarians to refine topics and search strategies (p. S.27, S.28).
- Online and In-Person Help: Assistance is available through the Public Service Desk or a live, non-bot online chat function (p. S.28).
- Interlibrary Loan: Through systems like MOBIUS and standard interlibrary loan, the library can acquire materials they do not personally own for students, free of charge (p. S.28).

In summary, the library is an essential partner in the academic experience, providing the tools and environment necessary to navigate the complex modern information landscape (p. S.26, S.29, S.30).

This summary covers the foundations of academic research as outlined in pages [S.31] through [S.46].

**Introduction to Source Types**
Academic research requires understanding different types of information sources. Rather than labeling them simply as "good" or "bad," researchers must evaluate them based on their specific research needs and the context in which the source was created. Academic expertise—defined by advanced degrees and research experience—is central to scholarly work [S.34].

**Popular vs. Scholarly Sources**
Popular sources, such as news articles or social media posts, are designed to entertain or inform the general public and are typically not written by experts [S.36-37]. In contrast, scholarly sources are created by academic experts to communicate original research or formal discourse to other scholars. Scholarly works are generally longer, include extensive citations, and are frequently peer-reviewed [S.38]. College-level research almost always benefits from the depth and rigour provided by scholarly sources [S.39].

**Scholarly Books**
Scholarly books provide in-depth analysis on niche topics. Unlike popular books, they often function as collections of essays where chapters may have different authors. Consequently, it is common practice to read and cite only the specific chapters relevant to a research project rather than the entire volume. Textbooks are generally excluded from this category as they are intended for student instruction rather than scholarly discourse [S.40-43].

**Journals and Journal Articles**
Journals are periodic publications that serve as the primary medium for researchers to exchange ideas and current findings. Getting an article published in a professional journal is a competitive process. Understanding the common structure of these articles—which often include sections like an introduction, methodology, and conclusion—helps researchers read more efficiently and aids in the citation process [S.44-46].

This text provides a guide on navigating scholarly journal articles and understanding the peer review process.

### Anatomy of a Journal Article
Journal articles are written by experts for experts, which can make them challenging for students. Effective reading requires understanding two main categories:

**Metadata**
Metadata refers to essential information needed for citations, including the article title, journal title, publication date, volume and issue numbers, page numbers, and the Digital Object Identifier (DOI) (p. 48).

**Structure and Reading Strategy**
Articles in the sciences and social sciences are highly structured, allowing readers to consume sections out of order depending on their specific needs (p. 50, 54):
1. Abstract: A short summary used to determine if the article is relevant (p. 50).
2. Introduction/Literature Review: Provides background and identifies research gaps (p. 51).
3. Methods: Details how the study was conducted (p. 51).
4. Results: Displays the raw data and findings (p. 51).
5. Discussion: Interprets results and places them in context (p. 52).
6. Conclusion: Shares primary takeaways and implications (p. 52).
7. Bibliography: Lists the sources referenced by the authors (p. 52).

While you may read parts of an article for research purposes, you should read the entire text before citing it in an assignment (p. 53). Articles in the humanities are generally less rigidly structured and are often better read from start to finish (p. 50).

### Peer Review
Peer review is the process by which research is evaluated by subject-matter experts before publication (p. 55).

Key traits of peer review:
- It serves as the "gold standard" of scholarly publications, ensuring that the research uses sound methodology and presents original, high-quality ideas (p. 55-56).
- It acts as an additional layer of verification beyond standard editorial review (p. 55).
- Students are often required to seek out specifically peer-reviewed articles for their assignments (p. 56).

This section explores "other" source types beyond popular and scholarly materials, specifically focusing on reference sources and government information.

**Reference Sources**
Reference sources, such as encyclopedias, dictionaries, and textbooks, provide essential background information, timelines, and vocabulary definitions [S.57, S.58]. While these are useful for brainstorming and understanding new topics, they are generally considered common knowledge and should not be used as primary citations in academic assignments unless directly quoted [S.58].

**The Role of Wikipedia**
Wikipedia functions as both a reference and a popular source [S.58]. Because it is crowd-sourced by volunteer editors rather than undergoing a traditional editorial process, it is prone to inaccuracies and occasional "vandalism" [S.58, S.59]. While its reliability has improved, researchers should approach it with caution and skepticism [S.58].

**Government Information**
This category encompasses any data, laws, policies, or reports produced by federal, state, or local government entities [S.60]. Government sources are highly valuable for specific research needs, such as:
- Demographic data (e.g., census records).
- Administrative regulations and laws.
- Multimedia material (e.g., NASA image archives).
- Local planning documents.

To find these resources, researchers are encouraged to visit specific $.gov$ websites or use search engine operators like $site:gov$ [S.60].

### Summary of Part III: Exploring the Information Landscape (pp. 61–92)

This section focuses on the practical tools and strategies required to navigate the modern information landscape effectively.

**Understanding Google and Search Tools**
While Google is a primary entry point for information, users must understand its limitations. Research involves more than a simple search bar; it requires knowing how to leverage both the broad capabilities of the open web and the specialized, organized structures of library databases (p. 63). 

**Library Resources and Databases**
The library acts as a gateway to high-quality information that is often unavailable or hidden behind paywalls on the free internet. Key resources include:

1. Databases: These are curated collections of scholarly and professional materials, organized to allow for precision searching that general internet engines cannot replicate (p. 78).
2. Advanced Access: When a library does not hold a specific item, services like MOBIUS and Interlibrary Loan (ILL) allow students to borrow materials from other institutions, bridging the gap between local holdings and global scholarly collections (p. 81).
3. Google Scholar: This tool serves as a bridge between the convenience of web searching and the rigor of scholarly materials, though it still requires careful navigation to access full-text content through institutional subscriptions (p. 85).

**Information Management**
Research is an iterative process where searching and evaluating occur simultaneously. A critical component of this workflow is proper documentation. Creating accurate citations is essential for maintaining the integrity of the scholarly conversation, ensuring that credit is given where due and that readers can trace sources independently (p. 88). 

Ultimately, this portion of the text emphasizes that successful research requires a balanced approach, utilizing library-specific tools for academic depth while maintaining a critical awareness of how different search platforms influence the results one finds (p. 72).

Part IV (p. 93-118) through Part VI (p. 147-174) of the text cover the following key areas:

Source Evaluation
Effective research requires critical assessment of materials. Students utilize the SIFT method to evaluate web sources and establish criteria for judging the expertise of authors. A core discussion explores whether true neutrality is possible in academic writing or if all sources carry inherent perspectives (p. 93-118).

History of Libraries and Information
This section examines the evolution of information storage, comparing pre-internet methods to the digital age. It highlights that the format of information often influences how it is consumed and understood. The text emphasizes that historical context is essential for navigating the current information landscape (p. 119-146).

The Information Economy
This part details the infrastructure behind scholarly communications, focusing on the roles of publishers and the impact of the paywall versus open access movements. Key legal frameworks for sharing information are also explained, specifically relating to copyright, interlibrary loan systems, and Creative Commons licensing (p. 147-174).

The final sections of the textbook address the critical intersection of technology and information management. 

Generative AI
This section identifies key concepts and ethical considerations regarding Artificial Intelligence in research (p. 175). It explicitly highlights the limitations of using AI for academic inquiry, emphasizing the importance of critical engagement when leveraging these tools (p. 183).

Citation Management
The text introduces Zotero as a primary tool for efficient citation management, designed to help students organize their research materials effectively (p. 199, 201). This section serves as a practical conclusion to the course's technical training, transitioning from conceptual understanding to the application of institutional research standards.

Final Student Output
The book concludes by showcasing the "Fall 2025 Student Bibliographies," which consist of sources annotated by undergraduates as part of their final project (p. 205). This serves as a practical implementation of the course's core objective: participating in the scholarly conversation (p. 1).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'scholarly conversation' as defined in the lesson?
options: A continuous process where researchers build upon, respond to, and share findings with one another.
A formal debate held by professors to determine which theories are legally allowed.
A static collection of facts that researchers memorize and repeat.
A private dialogue between an author and their publisher to ensure accuracy.
questions.explanation: The text describes research as a process where experts share findings, read each other's work, and use that information to develop new research, effectively continuing a conversation.

questions.question: Why is it important for a student to understand the 'information landscape'?
options: To ensure that they only ever use information that is free of charge.
To avoid having to ever use Google or other non-library tools.
To critically evaluate the complex, sometimes invisible, social and economic structures that influence information.
To learn how to memorize the location of every book in the university library.
questions.explanation: The text explains that the information landscape is complex and influenced by various structures; understanding these helps students navigate and evaluate information thoughtfully.

questions.question: What is the primary role of an 'Open Educational Resource' (OER)?
options: To provide teaching materials that are freely available to the public for use, adaptation, and redistribution.
To limit access to academic content to only students enrolled in a university.
To provide a paid service that helps students manage their citations automatically.
To ensure that all research is peer-reviewed by experts before it is published online.
questions.explanation: OER are defined as teaching materials that are freely available, with licenses that allow for free use, adaptation, and redistribution.

questions.question: If you encounter a paywall while searching for a research article, what is the best recommended action?
options: Contact the library to see if they already provide access or can obtain it through other means.
Pay the fee immediately to ensure you have the correct source for your assignment.
Look for an alternative version of the article on social media platforms.
Assume the article is not reputable since it is behind a paywall and find a different topic.
questions.explanation: The lesson advises students not to pay for access, as the library likely pays for it or can obtain it through services like interlibrary loan.

questions.question: How should a student approach the steps of the research process?
options: As a strict, linear set of rules that must be followed in order to avoid academic penalties.
As an automated process that is mostly handled by search engines and library databases.
As a flexible process where it is common to move back and forth between steps as new knowledge is discovered.
As a one-time task that is completed once the initial topic is selected and approved.
questions.explanation: The text notes that saying research happens in 'steps' is an oversimplification, and in reality, researchers often move back and forth between these stages as they learn more.

cards.front: What does OER stand for in the context of academic materials?
cards.back: Open Educational Resources, which are teaching materials that are freely available to the public for use, adaptation, and redistribution.
Research is the active process of brainstorming, searching, evaluating, and synthesizing information to engage with a topic and build knowledge.

cards.front: List the typical steps of the research process.
cards.back: Choosing a topic, getting background information, finding sources, evaluating sources, synthesizing information, and citing sources.
The ongoing process where researchers share findings, read each other's work, and build upon previous knowledge to contribute to a larger body of academic work.

cards.front: What is a citation and why is it mandatory?
cards.back: A citation is a reference to a source; it is mandatory to give credit to others' contributions, prove the validity of your claims, and allow readers to locate your sources.
Plagiarism is the act of including information from outside sources without providing proper credit or attempting to pass someone else's work off as your own.
A barrier that requires payment to access information, such as scholarly articles, which are often provided for free to students through their university library.

cards.front: Define 'Open Access' (OA) in the context of information.
cards.back: Open Access refers to scholarly research and other materials that are made freely available online to the public.

cards.front: What are the three core roles of a university library?
cards.back: Collecting/providing access to reliable materials, guiding students on how to find and navigate sources, and providing physical space for study and collaboration.

cards.front: Why is the research process described as an 'iceberg'?
cards.back: Because it appears simple on the surface but involves a vast, complex, and unseen depth of information structures, social norms, and economic factors.

slides.title: College Research Foundations
slides.notes: Welcome to the research foundations course, designed to help you navigate information and build essential academic skills.

slides.title: Welcome and Research Foundations
bullets: Understand course goals and library roles (p. 5)
Define research as an active process (p. 11)
Distinguish between paywalled and open sources (p. 17-18)
slides.notes: Research is an active process of discovery. We will explore the information landscape and how the library supports your academic journey.

slides.title: The Scholarly Conversation
bullets: We will master citation styles this term (p. 15)
slides.notes: Citations invite you into the 'scholarly conversation,' where you acknowledge others' work while contributing your own research.

slides.title: Foundations of Academic Research
bullets: Popular aims to entertain/inform the public (p. 28)
Learn to use sources suitable for college (p. 30)
slides.notes: Choosing the right source type is critical. We define the difference between popular media and scholarly, expert-vetted research.

slides.title: Anatomy of a Journal Article
bullets: Abstracts provide a quick article overview (p. 42)
Articles are often peer-reviewed by experts (p. 30, 42)
slides.notes: Journal articles can be dense, but understanding their anatomy—like the abstract, methods, and results—makes them much more accessible.

slides.title: Beyond Popular and Scholarly
bullets: Wikipedia is useful but needs verification (p. 50)
slides.notes: Beyond standard articles, reference materials and government sources provide essential foundational knowledge and raw data for research.
